frameworks.name: AIDA
frameworks.description: Attention-Interest-Desire-Action. The foundational framework for guiding prospects through complete decision-making process.
frameworks.when_to_use: Landing pages, sales letters, email campaigns, and ads where you can develop all four stages in single piece of content
steps: Attention: Hook with compelling headline or opening that stops scroll
Interest: Build relevance with specific benefits or intriguing information
Desire: Create emotional connection through transformation or fear of missing out
Action: Clear, specific call-to-action with urgency or incentive
frameworks.example: Attention: 'Your SaaS website is stopping you from doubling your MRR' → Interest: 'Here's why 73% of B2B buyers leave within 8 seconds' → Desire: 'Imagine closing 40% more deals with the same traffic' → Action: 'Get your free conversion audit in 24 hours'

frameworks.name: PAS
frameworks.description: Problem-Agitate-Solution. Most reliable approach for selling by starting with customer pain points.
frameworks.when_to_use: Short-form content: social media ads, email subject lines, blog post openings, cold outreach
steps: Problem: Identify specific, relatable pain point your audience faces
Agitate: Amplify the consequences and emotional cost of inaction
Solution: Present your offer as the logical resolution
frameworks.example: Problem: 'Managing sales pipeline with spreadsheets costs you 2 hours daily' → Agitate: 'While competitors close deals, you're updating cells and losing prospects to slow follow-up' → Solution: 'Switch to our AI-powered CRM and automate your workflow in 3 clicks'

frameworks.name: BAB
frameworks.description: Before-After-Bridge. Transformation-focused framework showing clear journey from current state to desired outcome.
frameworks.when_to_use: Email marketing, nurture sequences, transformational products, coaching services
steps: Before: Paint current frustrating situation with specific details
After: Describe vivid picture of improved life or business state
Bridge: Position your solution as the path between before and after
frameworks.example: Before: 'You're spending 15 hours weekly on manual reporting' → After: 'Imagine having all metrics auto-generated in real-time dashboards' → Bridge: 'Our analytics platform becomes your bridge to data-driven decisions in 48 hours'

frameworks.name: PPPP
frameworks.description: Problem-Promise-Proof-Proposal. Evidence-heavy framework for skeptical audiences requiring credibility.
frameworks.when_to_use: B2B sales pages, high-ticket offers, industries with trust issues, competitive markets
steps: Problem: Define specific business challenge with quantified impact
Promise: Make clear, measurable commitment to results
Proof: Provide concrete evidence through case studies, testimonials, data
Proposal: Present clear next step with risk reversal
frameworks.example: Problem: 'B2B companies lose $62K annually to poor lead qualification' → Promise: 'We'll increase your qualified leads by 150% in 90 days' → Proof: 'TechCorp saw 240% increase in 60 days (see case study)' → Proposal: 'Start your 30-day pilot with money-back guarantee'

frameworks.name: FAB
frameworks.description: Features-Advantages-Benefits. Product-focused framework translating specifications into customer value.
frameworks.when_to_use: Product descriptions, Google Ads, technical audiences, comparison content
steps: Features: List specific product capabilities or specifications
Advantages: Explain how features work better than alternatives
Benefits: Connect advantages to emotional or business outcomes
frameworks.example: Feature: 'AI-powered lead scoring algorithm' → Advantage: 'Processes 10,000 leads in 2 minutes vs 40 hours manually' → Benefit: 'Your sales team focuses on deals worth $50K+ instead of cold prospects'

frameworks.name: Eugene Schwartz Awareness Levels
frameworks.description: Five stages of customer awareness from Eugene Schwartz's 'Breakthrough Advertising'. Match your copy intensity and approach to where the prospect sits on the awareness spectrum.
frameworks.when_to_use: Full-funnel campaigns, email sequences, retargeting ads, and any campaign where you serve multiple audience temperatures simultaneously
frameworks.best_for: Email nurture sequences, segmented ad campaigns, landing page variants, long-form sales pages targeting mixed-awareness traffic
steps: Unaware: Prospect doesn't know they have a problem. Lead with story, entertainment, or pattern interrupt. Never mention the product. Open with relatable scenario or shocking statistic that surfaces latent pain.
Problem-Aware: Prospect feels the pain but doesn't know solutions exist. Agitate the problem with specifics. Quantify the cost of inaction. Build urgency around the pain without pitching yet.

Solution-Aware: Prospect knows solutions exist but doesn't know your product. Educate on solution categories. Position your approach as the superior method. Differentiate mechanism (how it works) not just outcomes.

Product-Aware: Prospect knows your product but hasn't bought. Overcome objections. Stack proof (testimonials, case studies, guarantees). Compare directly to alternatives. Address the specific reason they haven't converted yet.
Most-Aware: Prospect knows your product and wants it. Lead with the offer — discount, bonus, deadline. Minimal copy needed. Focus on CTA, pricing, urgency, and reducing friction to purchase.
frameworks.example: Unaware: 'The average family spends 47 weekends a year doing nothing memorable' → Problem-Aware: 'You keep saying you'll do something special this weekend — but Saturday arrives and you're back on the couch' → Solution-Aware: 'Immersive exhibitions are the fastest-growing family entertainment category for a reason' → Product-Aware: 'The Harry Potter Exhibition has sold 2M+ tickets across 12 cities — here's what 4,800 five-star reviews say' → Most-Aware: 'Last 3 weeks in Barcelona. Book before Friday and save 20%.'

frameworks.name: AICPBSAWN (Gary Halbert)
frameworks.description: Gary Halbert's 9-step direct response sequence. The most thorough persuasion chain for long-form sales copy, covering every psychological trigger from first glance to final close.
frameworks.when_to_use: Long-form sales letters, high-ticket landing pages, webinar follow-up sequences, comprehensive email campaigns
frameworks.best_for: High-ticket offers, product launches, direct mail campaigns, VSL (video sales letter) scripts, and any offer requiring full persuasion arc
steps: Attention: Interrupt pattern with bold headline, shocking stat, or provocative question. You have 3 seconds.
Interest: Expand on the hook with a story, relevant data, or 'what if' scenario that pulls reader deeper.
Credibility: Establish why you (or the brand) have authority. Credentials, media mentions, years in business, relevant expertise.
Prove: Back every claim with evidence — testimonials, case studies, screenshots, data, before/after results.
Benefits: Stack benefits using the 'which means' technique. Feature → advantage → emotional benefit. List at least 7-10.
Scarcity: Create genuine urgency — limited quantity, deadline, exclusive access, rising prices, seasonal relevance.
Action: Make the CTA crystal clear. Tell them exactly what to do, what happens next, and how quickly they get results.
Warn: Describe what happens if they don't act. Paint the cost of inaction in vivid, specific terms.
Now: Final push with immediacy. Restate the best benefit + scarcity. Make 'right now' feel like the only logical choice.
frameworks.example: Attention: 'This exhibition sells out 3 weeks before closing — every single city' → Interest: 'Over 2 million people have already walked through these doors' → Credibility: 'Created by the team behind the world's most visited touring exhibitions' → Prove: '4,800 five-star reviews. 94% say it exceeded expectations.' → Benefits: 'Interactive wand experience, exclusive photo moments, behind-the-scenes access' → Scarcity: 'Barcelona dates: March 15 - April 30 only. No extensions.' → Action: 'Select your date and book now at theimagineteam.com' → Warn: 'Last 3 cities sold out 2 weeks early. Late bookers paid 40% more on resale sites.' → Now: 'Tickets are live. Early-bird pricing ends this Friday.'

frameworks.name: Star-Story-Solution
frameworks.description: Narrative-driven framework that uses a relatable protagonist and story arc to deliver the solution. The 'star' creates identification, the 'story' builds emotional investment, and the 'solution' converts.
frameworks.when_to_use: Brand storytelling, case study content, video ads, testimonial-based campaigns, social media narrative posts
frameworks.best_for: Experience-based products, lifestyle brands, customer success stories, video content, influencer collaborations, exhibition marketing
steps: Star: Introduce the protagonist — a customer, founder, or relatable character the audience sees themselves in. Make them specific and vivid (name, situation, desire).
Story: Tell the journey — the problem they faced, the frustration, the turning point when they discovered the solution. Use sensory details and emotional beats. Build tension before the resolution.
Solution: Reveal how the product/experience transformed the star's situation. Show specific results. Connect back to the audience: 'You can have this too.' End with clear CTA.
frameworks.example: Star: 'Meet Sofia, 34, a Barcelona mom of two who hasn't had a magical family day in months' → Story: 'Every weekend was the same — park, screen time, repeat. She wanted her kids to feel wonder, not just be entertained. Then a friend sent her one photo from inside the exhibition...' → Solution: 'Three hours later, her 7-year-old was casting spells, her 4-year-old was sorting houses, and Sofia had 47 new photos on her phone. Book your family's magical day now.'

frameworks.name: 4 U's Formula
frameworks.description: Four-filter headline and hook optimization framework. Every piece of copy must pass through all four filters: Useful, Urgent, Unique, and Ultra-specific. Originally from Michael Masterson for email subject lines, now applied to all hooks.
frameworks.when_to_use: Headlines, email subject lines, ad copy hooks, social media opening lines, push notifications, banner ads
frameworks.best_for: Short-form copy optimization, A/B test headline creation, email marketing, paid ad headlines, any copy where the first line determines success
steps: Useful: Does this offer clear value? What will the reader gain, learn, or avoid? If there's no tangible takeaway, rewrite.
Urgent: Is there a reason to act now? Add time pressure, consequence of delay, or 'breaking' relevance. Without urgency, even great copy gets bookmarked and forgotten.
Unique: Has the audience seen this angle before? Differentiate through unexpected framing, contrarian take, or proprietary data. Avoid cliches at all costs.
Ultra-specific: Replace every vague word with a precise one. Numbers, names, timeframes, percentages. Specificity is the currency of believability.
frameworks.example: Weak: 'Great exhibition coming to town soon' → 4 U's applied: 'The Harry Potter Exhibition has 47 days left in Barcelona — 73% of weekend slots already sold. Book your specific date before your preferred time disappears.' (Useful: tells them what + where. Urgent: 47 days + 73% sold. Unique: specific exhibition detail. Ultra-specific: numbers throughout.)

frameworks.name: Before-During-After (BDA)
frameworks.description: Transformation journey framework that adds the critical 'During' phase missing from BAB. Shows the full arc: where you are, what the experience is like, and who you become after. Especially powerful for experiential products.
frameworks.when_to_use: Experience marketing, event promotion, course/program sales, travel and hospitality, exhibition and entertainment advertising
frameworks.best_for: Exhibitions, immersive experiences, events, retreats, destination marketing, any product where the experience IS the value proposition
steps: Before: Establish the current emotional state and situation. What does the audience's ordinary world look like? What are they missing or craving? Use relatable, specific details.

During: This is the differentiator — describe the experience itself in vivid, sensory detail. What do they see, feel, hear? Create FOMO by making the 'during' so compelling that not participating feels like a loss. Use present tense for immersion.
After: Show the lasting transformation. How do they feel after? What do they take away (memories, photos, stories, status)? This is the emotional payoff that justifies the purchase decision.
frameworks.example: Before: 'Another weekend scrolling through everyone else's adventures on Instagram while you plan nothing' → During: 'You're walking through Hogwarts corridors, wand in hand. Your kids are screaming with joy at the Quidditch zone. You're taking photos you'll actually frame.' → After: 'Monday morning, your coworkers ask about your weekend. This time, you have a story worth telling — and 50 photos to prove it.'

frameworks.name: Ticket-Selling Formula
frameworks.description: AUROS proprietary framework designed specifically for exhibition, event, and experience ticket sales. Optimized for the unique psychology of experiential purchase decisions where FOMO and visual impact drive conversion.
frameworks.when_to_use: Exhibition landing pages, event ticket campaigns, experience-based product launches, entertainment venue marketing, touring show promotions
frameworks.best_for: Exhibition ticket sales, concert/show promotion, museum campaigns, immersive experience marketing, themed event advertising, festival ticket drives
steps: Hook (Visual Wow): Lead with the most visually stunning or emotionally striking moment from the experience. Use hero imagery or video that creates instant desire. The visual must make someone stop and say 'I want to be there.'

Experience Preview: Give a curated walkthrough of 3-5 highlight moments they'll experience. Use sensory language — what they'll see, touch, hear, feel. Create a mental movie of attendance. Structure as a journey, not a feature list.

Social Proof (Numbers): Stack hard evidence — total visitors across cities, star ratings, review counts, media coverage, celebrity endorsements, sold-out history. Numbers create safety: 'If 2 million people loved it, I will too.'

Scarcity (Limited Dates): Emphasize the temporary nature of the experience. Specific closing date, percentage of tickets remaining, sold-out history from previous cities. Touring exhibitions have natural scarcity — use it aggressively.

CTA (Book Now): Direct, friction-free call to action. Include price anchoring if possible (value vs. cost comparison). Make the next step obvious — select date, choose tickets, checkout. Add urgency reinforcement in the button copy itself.
frameworks.example: Hook: [Hero image of illuminated Hogwarts Great Hall] 'Step inside the world you've only imagined' → Experience Preview: 'Walk the corridors of Hogwarts. Cast real spells with interactive wands. Meet Buckbeak up close. Brew potions in Snape's dungeon. Explore 5,000+ authentic props and costumes.' → Social Proof: '2.1M visitors. 4.8-star rating across 4,800 reviews. Sold out in London, New York, and Tokyo.' → Scarcity: 'Barcelona: March 15 - April 30 ONLY. Weekend slots are 73% sold. No extensions confirmed.' → CTA: 'Choose Your Date & Book Now — from 24.95. Early-bird family packs save 30%.'

key_principles.explanation: Specific numbers, timeframes, and outcomes create believability. '73% of users' beats 'most users'.
key_principles.application: Replace vague claims with precise metrics. Instead of 'increase sales,' write 'boost revenue by 34% in 90 days based on client average.'

key_principles.explanation: Anticipate and neutralize resistance points within your copy flow to maintain momentum.
key_principles.application: Include phrases like 'Even if you've tried other solutions' or 'No technical skills required' to preempt common concerns.

key_principles.explanation: Break expected patterns to recapture attention and create memorable moments.
key_principles.application: Start with contrarian statements: 'Most marketing advice is wrong. Here's what actually works...' or use unexpected formatting breaks.

key_principles.principle: Create urgency through scarcity or consequence
key_principles.explanation: Humans act faster when facing loss or limited availability than when pursuing gain.
key_principles.application: Use deadline-driven urgency ('Offer expires Friday') or consequence-based urgency ('Every day you wait costs $500 in lost revenue').

key_principles.principle: Match copy complexity to audience sophistication
key_principles.explanation: Aware audiences need different messaging than unaware prospects. Adjust depth accordingly.
key_principles.application: For aware audiences: focus on differentiation. For unaware: focus on problem education. For most aware: focus on offer details.

tactics.description: Use psychologically-proven words that trigger emotional response and action
tactics.expected_result: 15-25% increase in engagement rates when used strategically

tactics.description: Layer multiple types of social proof (numbers, testimonials, logos) within single piece
tactics.expected_result: Up to 34% improvement in conversion rates

tactics.description: Connect features to advantages to emotional benefits in logical sequence
tactics.expected_result: Higher perceived value and purchase intent

tactics.description: Create information gaps that compel audience to take action for resolution
tactics.expected_result: 20-40% higher click-through rates on subject lines and headlines

tactics.description: Transfer purchase risk from buyer to seller through guarantees and trials
tactics.expected_result: 25-60% increase in conversion rates for high-ticket offers

templates.name: PAS Email Template
templates.template:
Subject: [SPECIFIC PROBLEM] is costing you [QUANTIFIED LOSS]

Hi [NAME],

I noticed [SPECIFIC OBSERVATION ABOUT THEIR BUSINESS].

Here's the problem: [CLEAR PROBLEM STATEMENT]

This means [CONSEQUENCE 1], [CONSEQUENCE 2], and worst of all, [BIGGEST CONSEQUENCE].

The solution? [YOUR OFFER IN ONE SENTENCE]

[SOCIAL PROOF OR RESULT]

Ready to [DESIRED OUTCOME]?

[CLEAR CTA]

[SIGNATURE]
templates.use_case: Cold outreach, product launch emails, problem-aware audiences

templates.name: BAB Social Media Ad
templates.template:
BEFORE: You're [CURRENT FRUSTRATING SITUATION]

AFTER: Imagine [SPECIFIC DESIRED OUTCOME WITH NUMBERS]

The bridge? [YOUR SOLUTION]

✅ [BENEFIT 1]
✅ [BENEFIT 2] 
✅ [BENEFIT 3]

[SOCIAL PROOF]

👇 [CTA] (Link in comments)
templates.use_case: Facebook/Instagram ads, LinkedIn posts, transformation-focused content

templates.name: AIDA Landing Page Header
templates.template:
HEADLINE: [ATTENTION-GRABBING CLAIM WITH NUMBER]

SUBHEAD: [INTEREST-BUILDING EXPLANATION]

Imagine [DESIRE-CREATING SCENARIO]

[CTA BUTTON: ACTION-ORIENTED TEXT]

[TRUST INDICATOR/SOCIAL PROOF]
templates.use_case: Landing pages, sales pages, lead magnets

common_mistakes: Leading with features instead of benefits
Using industry jargon that confuses prospects
Using generic social proof instead of specific results
Writing for everyone instead of ideal customer
Burying the main benefit in body copy
Neglecting mobile readability and scanning patterns
pro_tips: Test emotional vs logical appeals for your specific audience - B2B often responds better to logical frameworks with emotional undertones
Use 'you' 3x more than 'we' or 'I' to maintain customer focus throughout copy
Place strongest benefit in first 8 words of headlines for maximum impact
Create curiosity gaps in subject lines but deliver on promises in body copy
Use power words strategically: 'secret,' 'proven,' 'guaranteed,' 'exclusive' increase engagement by 20%+
Structure paragraphs in 1-2-1 pattern: short opener, longer explanation, short closer for optimal readability
Include specific timeframes in promises ('in 30 days' vs 'quickly') to increase credibility
Use transitional phrases to maintain flow: 'Here's why,' 'But here's the thing,' 'The result?'
End sections with forward momentum: questions, cliffhangers, or benefit previews
A/B test framework choice - PAS often outperforms AIDA for problem-aware audiences by 15-30%
